items.q: Does the program counter hold the instruction currently being executed?
items.correct: No - the PC holds the ADDRESS of the next instruction to fetch; the fetched instruction itself is held in the current instruction register.
items.misconception: Yes - the program counter contains the instruction being run.
items.random_wrong: Yes - the program counter counts completed instructions.
items.why: Address and content are constantly confused. The PC is a pointer into memory, always one step ahead, which is exactly how jumps work - by overwriting it.

items.q: Does the accumulator store memory addresses?
items.correct: No - the accumulator holds data, specifically the results of ALU operations; addresses are held in the PC and MAR.
items.misconception: Yes - the accumulator accumulates the addresses of used memory.
items.random_wrong: Yes - the accumulator is part of the hard drive.
items.why: Registers blur into one 'CPU storage'. Assign each its cargo: addresses travel via PC and MAR, data and results via MDR and the accumulator.

items.q: Are the address bus and the data bus the same set of wires?
items.correct: No - they are separate buses: the address bus says WHERE in memory to look, while the data bus carries WHAT is being transferred.
items.misconception: Yes - one shared bus carries both addresses and data simultaneously.
items.random_wrong: Yes - buses are software, not physical connections.
items.why: Both connect CPU and memory, so they merge. Separating where (address), what (data) and how (control) is the standard three-bus model exams expect.

items.q: After an instruction is fetched, what normally happens to the program counter?
items.correct: It is incremented to hold the address of the next instruction
items.random_wrong: It stores the result of the calculation
items.why: It's It is incremented to hold the address of the next instruction. “It is wiped to zero” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.correct: Data or instructions just read from, or about to be written to, memory
items.misconception: The address of the next instruction
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Data or instructions just read from, or about to be written to, memory. “The address of the next instruction” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: The von Neumann bottleneck refers to:
items.correct: The shared bus between processor and memory limiting the rate of data transfer
items.misconception: The small size of modern keyboards
items.why: Correct answer: The shared bus between processor and memory limiting the rate of data transfer. “The small size of modern keyboards” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Read and write signals are carried by which bus?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: The control bus. “The address bus” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: A system has a 16-bit address bus. How many distinct memory locations can it address (2 to the power 16)?
items.why: Exact answer: 65536 locations. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
